item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I came here to ask this too - Obviously its always author preference, but I've always liked it when SCP's that reference dates in the past like this keep continuity with the real world. It helps with immersion imo.

Either way, I +1'd. Fantastic article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Time to update the sad lobster to neutralized?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seriously! You know, this problem could literally be fixed if the article(s) just said they used SCP-2154 to view the anomaly.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - Wikiwalk
item.content:encoded: Works for me.

item.title: Junior Staff Post - Wikiwalk
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk Team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

This SCP is mentioned as violating the Square-Cube Law. A quick venue for crosslinks would be to add a footnote saying the mechanism by which it does this is similar to other SCPs that are mentioned as doing the same (which will be similar in content, having giant animals.)

Changing this:

This fluid also heats up during energy transportation, generating enough heat to keep SCP-3485 alive, essentially negating the Square Cube Law.

To this:

This fluid also heats up during energy transportation, generating enough heat to keep SCP-3485 alive, essentially negating the Square Cube Law1.

If anyone has any other ideas, feel free to suggest them below.

Footnotes
1. Research is being conducted to determine if SCP-2378 or SCP-3536 violate the Square Cube Law in a similar fashion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show gripes
- hide gripes

The primary food source of SCP-3485 is residue created by stars during fission reactions, such as leftover hydrogen and helium.

Stars get their energy from fusion, not fission. Also, the "residue" wording is strange; hydrogen and helium are just the bulk of what stars are made of, so calling them "residue created by stars" is a bit like calling water "residue created by the ocean".

feeding is accomplished using heat-resistant tendons located in its mouth.

I don't think "tendons" is the word you're looking for, although I'm no expert on lobster anatomy.

Thirdly, SCP-3485 has an efficient transportation system that negates the need for nutrients and oxygen. The bloodstream of SCP-3485 does not use blood, but rather a highly conductive gaseous solution to transport electrical energy. In addition to this, multiple high-efficiency recycling systems replace regular respiratory procedures responsible for life. This fluid also heats up during energy transportation, generating enough heat to keep SCP-3485 alive, essentially negating the Square Cube Law.

There are a whole bunch of problems with this passage:
1. "Highly conductive gaseous solution" is very dubious. Mixtures of gases are usually not called "solutions" (I'd probably just go with "gas mixture"), and no gas I know of is a remotely good medium for electric power transmission. If this is part of the anomaly, I think the article should say so. Given that this beastie tends to spend its time eating stars, I'd be willing to buy that it had blazing-hot plasma circulating in its arteries - perhaps it somehow anomalously generates high magnetic fields to confine the plasma into superhot streams, in a similar fashion to a Tokamak fusion reactor?
2. "Multiple high-efficiency recycling systems" is not very clear. What is being recycled? How does this replace respiration?
3. You have the square-cube law backwards. Bigger creatures have a harder time getting rid of heat, and overheat more easily from exertion, while tiny creatures more easily freeze. Also, you say that its circulatory system is highly efficient just a couple of sentences ago, which means specifically that it doesn't generate a lot of heat.
4. I'm confused as to how we know all of this. Is this what the 2016 mission mentioned later on found out? If so, the article doesn't appear to say so right now.

In multiple instances, collisions with exo-planets have left no signs of damage. In addition, SCP-3485 shows no signs of burns or radiation damage from its regular, extended contact with small stars.

11/28/2008: SCP-3485 reaches into the star, possibly in an attempt to recover SCP-3485-1. Its claws are burnt as a result.

This looks like a contradiction.

03/05/2016: Dr. ███████ notes the similarities between the area SCP-3485 inhabits and previous sites of Class C "Broken Entry" wormholes.

This reference to Class C "Broken Entry" wormholes seems without context. Is this supposed to be suggesting that it uses this kind of wormhole to move around, or that it looks like it tried to teleport and failed, or that it's out here waiting for one of these wormholes to appear, or something else entirely? We're not left with much, because there's no indication given of what makes this area look like previous Class C wormhole sites.

However, it is unknown how SCP-3485 does not collapse according to the rules of gravity, or how it is biologically immortal.

1. "Does not collapse according to the rules of gravity" is awkward wording. Perhaps "does not undergo gravitational collapse?" Also, it should be noted that there'd be nothing necessarily wrong with this skip existing at that size if it just happened to have a very low density compared to a normal lobster, which you may have already been hinting at with the gaseous circulatory system thing.
2. How do we know that it's biologically immortal? It doesn't sound like we've seen enough of these things to have any idea of what their lifespans are like, and worse still, we've seen a dead one that looks a lot like it died nonviolently. Maybe it didn't, who knows, but in the picture the Foundation has in-universe, that's not good evidence for biological immortality.

Voct is absolutely right about the speed-of-light problem. If this thing is indeed travelling faster than light, we should be seeing everything it does out of order. Indeed, travelling between locations instantaneously at an interstellar scale is necessarily a violation of causality as we know it, so it's probably impossible to say if these events happened in any particular order at all.

Finally, I want to note that our current telescopes can't make out any details at all over interstellar distances on the scale of star-sized objects. They're just too far away, and even the very largest diffraction-limited telescopes (that is, those operating at the limit of what's permitted by the quantum behavior of light for a given aperture size) can still only study other stars by looking at their spectra over time, and not by taking actual detailed pictures. If the Foundation is using some kind of paratechnology to get around this and take good pictures of remote star systems, this should be indicated in the article in some way, because it would be technology with groundbreaking implications.

Barring all of that, I do rather like the concept of space gigafauna, particularly in forms that resemble sea creatures. I'll be happy to upvote it once these problems are addressed in some way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He just wanted to be loved. *sniff*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You made me feel sad for an eldritch apocalypse-causing lobster :(

…take my upvote…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There seems to be a general problem with astronomical articles that don't seem to remember that light is slower than things defined as faster than it. This isn't the only one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That timeline is a problem, also. It's too linear. This thing jumps hundreds of light-years in all directions and does stuff. We should be seeing various incidents in its timeline completely out of sequence. We should be sighting it in multiple places at once, because the differing signals get to us at the same time. We should be continuing to get images of it appearing in other star systems even after we know that it has apparently given up and starved itself to death.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The changes are decent; what you could do is mention that when a teleportation event occurs, there's a flash of light similar in luminosity to a supernova and a surge of gamma radiation, or something along those lines, to indicate that wormholes are involves somehow. Perhaps when it created a worm hole at some time in its normal universe, something went wrong and it ended up in ours.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, this was pointed out by Voct, and replaced with teleportation.

item.title: Re: Also!
item.content:encoded: Fixed all of the errors you pointed out. Thank you for your help!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Giant sad space lobster is sad. :( +1 for sad.

That said, some of the language in this could stand tightening.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Let's say it is maintained by anomalous law to not collapse into a blackhole, assuming the various circulatory systems and gas sac halved its density from normal lobster density, how much would it probably weigh—and how much gas it would need to expel to accelerate itself to a reasonable pace within a solar system?

it would be a lobster that farts out planet-sized gas bubbles, which is comedic in its own way, but too ridiculous and impractical—it would exhaust its reserves long before it reach the edge of the system.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a downvote from me. It's a lobster that eats stars and do FTL, sure, but then again I played Stellaris last week and encountered at least two species of star eaters and two others that feeds on gas giants. The story of it finding carcass of its own kind is also too bland to my liking, it came out as forced attempt to add story to a complete article about weird creature.

And then there's the complaint about the incomplete physics that others has complained about. But then again, it's an anomaly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also, it doesn't need FTL at all.

It needs FTL to be "transporting" itself from star to star, apparently instantaneously, as stated in the log.

Expelling air as reaction mass has terrible ISP anyway. The physics here doesn't work at all (this thing should collapse in on itself, if it were as dense as a lobster at this size), so it's hard to really say how much thrust it would get, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"…created by stars during fission reactions, such as leftover hydrogen and helium. "

shouldn't that be "fusion"?

anyway, glad to see you took my suggestion and did your own thing with it, it's probably better than what I was suggesting. The incredibly lengthy story imo would have dragged this scip down, so taking that out was a good move. I still have the minor gripe I spoke about; while it may _look_ like a lobster, clearly this thing is quite different, and it doesn't appear that the Foundation was able to collect DNA samples.

For the most part though, I like it, so that's an upvote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Keter dictates containment difficulty. This thing is impossible to contain, and if it decided it wanted to nibble on our sun there would be nothing we could do to stop it. It has the potential to wipe out all life on earth, and we don't even register on its perceptual radar — therefore, Keter.

Also, it doesn't need FTL at all. 17 l.y. may mean it takes hundreds and hundreds of years to reach us, but that still makes it a massive threat. A fraction of lightspeed would be enough to get it here in a relatively timely fashion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So it's 17 light years away from Earth but regularly (and extremely rapidly) travels to the crab nebula thousands of light years away using only bursts of air?

Without explicit FTL ability this doesn't even begin to justify Keter classification.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I kind of like the idea of a big-ass space lobster just chilling there, but I do have a few gripes:

"Class-A amnestics are to be administered to remove memories of SCP-3485" sounds a little redundant. It's like saying, "I take sleeping pills to help me sleep."

But the lobster in the room for me is the fact that the Crab Nebula is over six thousand light-years away from Earth. This means that everything we observed took place right about when Egypt was dealing with a whole lot of bad shit coming out of the sky and a massive labor shortage in the pyramid business. This leads to two major problems.

First, all data regarding SCP-3485 is way out of date, including its current whereabouts. There is no point sending an expedition out there to try and save its life or make observations before it dies, because that already happened before a certain Indian man went on walkabout and accidentally became a god.

The second major problem is that even if the Foundation is hiding some wonderful tech from the rest of the world, that puts them maybe a century ahead of the curve. How are you planning on sending an expedition there at several thousand times the speed of light?

The way I see it, the easiest way to make the logistics work would be to piggyback onto some of the stuff established by events such as Project Heimdall. There are probably several SCPs or tales establishing FTL travel and communication if you choose to crosslink, but with no explanation there is no way to make an expedition work.

item.title: Lobster for Dinner, anyone?
item.content:encoded:
When I first joined this site, I expected to see nothing but terrifying monsters that go bump in the night, gruesome humanoids with fingernails for teeth and other utter horrors from the darkest abyss. But when I looked into the abyss, the big space lobster looked back.

+1

item.title: Also!
item.content:encoded:
To digest this material, SCP-3485 has three anomalous stomachs that immerse the material into a cooling solution, then ignites it in a poorly understood way to release its energy, This energy is absorbed by the stomach wall and transported throughout the body. This digestive system is capable of complete mass-energy conversion, and produces no waste products.

How does the Foundation know this? Have expeditions been inside the creature and counted its stomachs? Bear in mind that its stomachs would be larger than the Earth.

extra volume used to contain pressurized air. In order to travel through space, SCP-3485 opens multiple pores on its shell to release this air in bursts

… this creature is larger than the sun. Do you realize how much it would mass?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
asteroids over 10 kilometers in diameter moving at speeds over 50 kilometers per second have collided with SCP-3485 and left no signs of damage.

You've said that this thing is larger than the sun. An asteroid 10km in diameter would be less than a grain of sand to it. Traveling at 50 km/ second, it would take the asteroid over eight hours to pass from one end of the creature to the other. For 3485 to be immune to damage from such an asteroid is hardly anomalous.

Similarly

SCP-3485 was resistant to conventional drilling equipment, high-power lasers, and bombing.

Again, no shit: it's larger than the sun. Do you realize how thick its shell is? If we assume proportionality, and there's no reason we shouldn't, then at its thinnest the shell would be well over ten thousand kilometers thick. You expect to drill through that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Somebody went to a quasar
And there they saw a star
But it wasn't a star
It was a STAR LOBSTER!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An updoot for the Lobstar.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
If this seems familiar, this is a rewrite of a thing I used to have in the 3085 slot. Hopefully it's better now.

Thanks to Mendelssohn, MaliceAforethought, piceus, Jack Ike, taylor_itkin, Tufto, Modern_Erasmus, Doctor Cimmerian and other denizens of Site-19 who I do not remember for crit on the old draft. Thanks to Tanhony, mdvs, updogg, rockyred9, Gummy_Dragon, Lord_of_Laugh, psul, DrChandra, Doctor Cambrian, 9Volt and ModernMajorGeneral for critique on the new draft. Thanks to Roget for the name.

Image Sources:

The lobster: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Homarus#/media/File:Lobster.jpg

Alternative name for this SCP: Lobstar
